profile_bio.description: The entrepreneurship company

kept.text:
You hit a winning ad. Scale fast. Then your supplier can't keep up.

Can't fulfill orders in time. Shopify payment banned. Forced to turn ads off. Store dies.

Most of you panic and switch suppliers. 

That might not fix anything.

"My supplier can't keep up" tells you nothing. Figure out where the bottleneck actually is.

Your dropshipping supplier does 2 jobs: sourcing (the factory) and fulfillment (the 3PL).

You don't know which one broke.

If the factory is the problem - your agent is supposed to source more factories. That's their job. Hold them to it. If you're sourcing yourself, scale more factories.

If the 3PL (agent) is the problem - find one that's bigger with more automations. A new 3PL or a bigger supplier with a bigger fulfillment center.

This is why it's worth visiting China yourself. Verify your supply chain in person. Your stock doesn't magically appear at your customer's doorstep.

Two different problems. Two different fixes. If you're scaling turbo fast you're probably going to have to fix both.

Comfrt turbo scaled their brand all the way to $1B/year now and they had to scale factories + fulfillment super aggressively to keep up with demand.

While you're fixing it, put the store on preorder.

Real scarcity converts. We've held conversion rates at $10k, $70k, $100k days on preorder.

Delayed orders with zero communication is what actually kills you.

Chargebacks pile up. Payment processor holds your funds. Now you have two problems instead of one.

Hire more customer service reps. Be proactive with communication.

The goal is to keep the machine running while you fix the engine.

kept.text:
Average week in Ecom throughout the year:

Monday - Meta problem.
Tuesday - Chargebacks.
Wednesday - Shopify Down.
Thursday - Finally profitable.
Friday - "I can scale this to $100M"
Saturday - Inventory problem
Sunday - I’m done” -> launch 3 new campaigns.

I do ecom = losing money professionally

Most people replace what breaks. Entrepreneurs reimagine it.

Joe Traeger turned a backyard barbecue disaster into a billion-dollar category. 40 years later, @TraegerGrills still leads it.

They just launched on @Shopify. Welcome. https://t.co/00dtJ5OT1c

profile_bio.description: Entrepreneur // @Shopify President // @BigShotPodcast Creator

kept.text:
brands are getting shut down by Shopify payments at 0.1% chargeback rates, even if you have clean metrics, a stable business, and zero warning. If they decide your business is too risky, they will pull the plug.
 
here's what the payment stack should look like as you scale, built from the bottom up:
 
↳ tier 1, up to $10M/year - Shopify payments or Stripe (same underlying platform, Stripe powers Shopify payments).
 
works great until you skew a risk ratio, your supplier ships a product one or two weeks late, or you get a few disputes on quality. then it's a reserve, and a shutdown two or three weeks later with zero warning.
 
↳ tier 1 shutdown triggers - a supplement they no longer want to support, CBD or THC, subscriptions they don't want to support anymore, or anything they decide is too risky.
 
↳ tier 2, high-risk processing - where most $0-$10M/month operators land after a ban. fees are a little more expensive than shopify but the risk tolerances are higher and they support more products and business models.
 
↳ tier 3, past $10M/year - now you're a bigger fish and you can walk into the higher channels with leverage.
 
"i've been on high-risk processing for 6 months, account's stable, no big reserves, no chargebacks, $10M/month brand."
 
that unlocks payment facilitators like Adyen, Airwallex, and Stripe. cheaper rates similar to shopify, higher approvals, and more stable. below $10-15M/year in annual revenue, that door is just closed off to you.
 
↳ tier 4, $50M-$100M/year - building on Adyen and Airwallex as the main channels. still keeping the high-risk mids in the stack for any payments that decline or transactions they don't want sending to the main processors.
 
those route to the high-risk mids as a fallback.
 
when a brand gets shut down with zero warning, there are really only two options. 

tweet it out and hope somebody helps or Shopify magically reinstates you, or move to high-risk processing and start building the stack properly.
